2:0.rendering: This is now, beloved, the second letter I am writing to you. In both of them I stir up your sincere mind by way of reminder,
2:0.note: The Greek verb "diegeiro" means to fully awaken or rouse from sleep, like shaking someone out of slumber to remember.

2:1.rendering: so that you remember the words spoken beforehand by the holy prophets and the commandment of the Lord and Savior given through your apostles
2:1.note: This verse weaves the prophets and apostles together as one authoritative voice, an early hint of the emerging canon that holds the Bible in unified tension.

2:2.rendering: Know this first of all, that in the last days scoffers will come, scoffing as they follow their own desires.
2:2.note: The Greek word for "scoffers" (empaiktai) echoes the mocking that Jesus endured at his trial, linking end-time resistance to the pattern of rejecting God's messenger.

2:3.rendering: They will say, "Where is this promised coming of his? For ever since the ancestors fell asleep, all things have continued exactly as they were from the beginning of creation."
2:3.note: The scoffers echo the ancient complaint of the wicked in Psalm 10 and Malachi 2, weaponizing the apparent delay of judgment as proof that God is absent or powerless.

2:4.rendering: For this escapes their notice deliberately: that by the word of God heavens existed long ago and an earth was formed out of water and through water.
2:4.note: The phrase "escapes their notice deliberately" plays on a Greek word for willful ignorance, contrasting the scoffers' chosen blindness with the ancient creation account where God's word brings order from watery chaos.

2:5.rendering: By these the world that then existed was deluged with water and perished.
2:5.note: The Greek "kosmos" here echoes the flood narrative in Genesis, where the ordered world of Noah's day was overwhelmed by the very waters that once birthed creation.

2:6.rendering: But the present heavens and the earth are stored up by the same word, reserved for fire on the day of judgment and destruction of the ungodly.
2:6.note: The same word that once unleashed floodwaters now holds the cosmos in reserve for purifying fire, echoing the ancient promise that God's word both creates and judges.

2:7.rendering: But do not overlook this one thing, beloved: with the Lord one day is like a thousand years, and a thousand years are like one day.
2:7.note: This echoes Psalm 90:4, using vast time scales to reframe God's patience as purposeful mercy rather than delay.

2:8.rendering: The Lord is not slow about his promise as some regard slowness, but he is patient toward you, not wanting any to perish but all to come to repentance.
2:8.note: Peter echoes God's patient waiting in the flood story, showing the delay before judgment is mercy inviting return.

2:9.rendering: But the day of the Lord will come like a thief. The heavens will disappear with a roar, the elements will be destroyed by fire, and the earth and everything done in it will be laid bare.
2:9.note: The Greek word for "laid bare" (heurethesetai) carries the sense of being exposed or found out, echoing how God searches hearts in the ancient prophets.

2:10.rendering: Since all these things are going to be dissolved in this way, what sort of people must you be? You ought to live in holy conduct and godliness.
2:10.note: The Greek word for "what sort" echoes the same term used for the transformed character of Jesus' followers at the transfiguration, calling believers to reflect his glory amid the coming cosmic meltdown.

2:11.rendering: as you look forward to and speed the coming of the day of God, on account of which the heavens will be dissolved in fire and the elements will melt as they burn.
2:11.note: The rare verb "speed" echoes Isaiah 60:22, where God promises to hasten the appointed time.

2:12.rendering: But according to his promise, we are waiting for new heavens and a new earth, where righteousness dwells.
2:12.note: Peter echoes Isaiah 65:17 and 66:22, where the new creation arrives as God's final answer to injustice.

2:13.rendering: Therefore, beloved, while you wait for these things, be eager to be found by him without spot or blemish, and at peace.
2:13.note: The Greek word for "eager" here echoes the same verb used for the scoffers' mocking question about his coming in verse 4, turning their taunt into a call to active readiness.

2:14.rendering: And count the patience of our Lord as salvation, just as our beloved brother Paul also wrote to you according to the wisdom given him.
2:14.note: Peter links Paul's letters to the Lord's patient delay of judgment, an intertextual nod that treats them as authoritative Scripture.

2:15.rendering: He speaks about these things in all his letters. Some things in them are hard to understand, which the ignorant and unstable twist to their own destruction, as they do the other Scriptures.
2:15.note: Peter places Paul's letters alongside the other Scriptures, already treating them as authoritative writings in the first century.

2:16.rendering: You therefore, beloved, knowing this beforehand, be on your guard so that you are not carried away by the error of lawless people and lose your own stability.
2:16.note: The Greek word for "carried away" echoes the violent sweeping force of a flood or storm, a vivid warning against being swept off your feet by deceptive teaching.

2:17.rendering: But grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To him be the glory both now and to the day of eternity. Amen.
2:17.note: Peter closes his letter by urging believers to keep growing in grace and in knowing Jesus, echoing the letter's opening call to add virtue upon virtue so their knowledge doesn't remain static.
